repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem
Several new protocols that Neovim could leverage, such as [MCP](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/getting-started/intro) and [ACP](http://agentclientprotocol.com/), are based on JSON-RPC. While we already have a well-implemented JSON-RPC client, it is currently restricted for use only with LSP.

## Solution
Extract a generic RPC client, `vim.json.rpc`, from `vim.lsp.rpc`, which was previously dedicated solely to LSP.

```
Transport
    stdin/stdout | tcp | pipe
          |
          v
Message Stream? (optional)
    content-length | raw
          |
          v
JSON-RPC Peer
    decode JSON / dispatch / callbacks / encode JSON
          |
          v
LSP Client
    handle LSP requests / responses
```

## Roadmap
- [X] Clearer responsibilities for the transport layer. #38560
- [ ] Added message framing layer. #39000
- [X] No longer dependent on `tostring` for error formatting. #38707
- [ ] Generic logger based on `vim.lsp.log`. #38906
- [ ] A clearer way for error callbacks.
- [ ] (Partially) deprecate the `vim.lsp.rpc` module, follow-up.

comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -83,8 +83,8 @@ function TransportRun:terminate()
   assert(self.sysobj):kill(15)
 end
 
---- @class (private,exact) vim.lsp.rpc.Transport.Connect : vim.lsp.rpc.Transport
---- @field new fun(): vim.lsp.rpc.Transport.Connect
+--- @class (private,exact) vim..Transport.Connect : vim.Transport
+--- @field new fun(): vim..Transport.Connect

comment_data.body:
I understand the intention behind the name change. But, the name `on_request` sounds like a generic hook similar to the `on_attach` previously used on LSP.

I think that using `server_request` and `server_notify` would be clearer to signal the purpose of these functions. I'm somewhat familiar with LSP, but no with JSON-RPC. So, maybe these names do make sense in this context. In that case, would changing the names of the exposed `dispatchers` on the LSP side be an option? Calling `on_request` to trigger a server to client LSP request from an in-memory lua LSP server doesn't seem very intuitive to me (although I can see the appeal of `on_request` when using `dispatchers` as an interface to talk to external language servers, like TransportConnect does)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,574 @@
+local log = require('vim.lsp.log')
+local vim_transport = require('vim._transport')
+local strbuffer = require('vim._core.stringbuffer')
+local validate = vim.validate
+
+--- Embeds the given string into a table and correctly computes `Content-Length`.
+---
+--- @param message string
+--- @return string message with `Content-Length` attribute
+local function format_message_with_content_length(message)
+  return table.concat({
+    'Content-Length: ',
+    tostring(#message),
+    '\r\n\r\n',
+    message,
+  })
+end
+
+--- Extract `content-length` from the header.
+---
+--- The structure of header fields conforms to [HTTP semantics](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#section-3.2),
+--- i.e., `header-field = field-name : OWS field-value OWS`. OWS means optional whitespace (space/horizontal tabs).
+---
+--- We ignore lines ending with `\n` that don't contain `content-length`, since some servers
+--- write log to standard output and there's no way to avoid it.
+--- See https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/35743#pullrequestreview-3379705828
+--- @param header string The header to parse
+--- @return integer
+local function get_content_length(header)
+  local state = 'name'
+  local i, len = 1, #header
+  local j, name = 1, 'content-length'
+  local buf = strbuffer.new()
+  local digit = true
+  while i <= len do
+    local c = header:byte(i)
+    if state == 'name' then
+      if c >= 65 and c <= 90 then -- lower case
+        c = c + 32
+      end
+      if (c == 32 or c == 9) and j == 1 then -- luacheck: ignore 542
+        -- skip OWS for compatibility only
+      elseif c == name:byte(j) then
+        j = j + 1
+      elseif c == 58 and j == 15 then
+        state = 'colon'
+      else
+        state = 'invalid'
+      end
+    elseif state == 'colon' then
+      if c ~= 32 and c ~= 9 then -- skip OWS normally
+        state = 'value'
+        i = i - 1
+      end
+    elseif state == 'value' then
+      if c == 13 and header:byte(i + 1) == 10 then -- must end with \r\n
+        local value = buf:get()
+        if digit then
+          return vim._assert_integer(value)
+        end
+        error('value of Content-Length is not number: ' .. value)
+      else
+        buf:put(string.char(c))
+      end
+      if c < 48 and c ~= 32 and c ~= 9 or c > 57 then
+        digit = false
+      end
+    elseif state == 'invalid' then
+      if c == 10 then -- reset for next line
+        state, j = 'name', 1
+      end
+    end
+    i = i + 1
+  end
+  error('Content-Length not found in header: ' .. header)
+end
+
+local M = {}
+
+--- Mapping of error codes used by the client
+--- @enum vim.json.rpc.ClientErrors
+local client_errors = {
+  INVALID_SERVER_MESSAGE = 1,
+  INVALID_SERVER_JSON = 2,
+  NO_RESULT_CALLBACK_FOUND = 3,
+  READ_ERROR = 4,
+  NOTIFICATION_HANDLER_ERROR = 5,
+  SERVER_REQUEST_HANDLER_ERROR = 6,
+  SERVER_RESULT_CALLBACK_ERROR = 7,
+}
+
+--- @type table<string,integer> | table<integer,string>
+--- @nodoc
+M.client_errors = vim.deepcopy(client_errors)
+for k, v in pairs(client_errors) do
+  M.client_errors[v] = k
+end
+
+--- Dispatchers for incoming JSON-RPC message types.
+--- @class vim.json.rpc.Dispatchers
+--- @inlinedoc
+--- @field on_notify fun(method: string, params: table?)
+--- @field on_request fun(method: string, params: table?): any?, vim.json.rpc.Error?

comment_data.body:
The problem here is that 'server_request' and 'server_notify' are incorrect terminology in the context of JSON-RPC. In the terminology of JSON-RPC, a client is defined as the one that requests and notifies, and a server is defined as the one that responds. For JSON-RPC, the server never sends requests or notifications.

It is important to note that the client and server in LSP and the client and server in JSON-RPC do not correspond. The client in LSP is both a JSON-RPC server and a client, and the same is true for the server.

Take an example. If we use two nvim to establish a connection via JSON-RPC as follows
```
      rquest
nvim1 -----> nvim2
```
The protocol we implement here may just allow nvim1 to call nvim2's functions via JSON-RPC, and this function is not bidirectional. In our protocol, nvim1 is the client and nvim2 is the server.
When we code for nvim1, we can use `request` for sending a request, but when we code for nvim2, we write a `server_request` for responding, but this responds to nvim1, and nvim1 is a client. Isn't that confusing?

The key problem is that the rpc object we are implementing is neither a JSON-RPC client (because it can respond to requests) nor a JSON-RPC server (because it can send requests). Any attempt to describe the rpc objects here in terms of client and server is confusing. According to my research, in other JSON-RPC libraries, such objects are also called 'ClientAndServer' or 'Connection'.

If 'on_request' is not clear enough, we can call it 'on_request_receive'. In conclusion, I'm **very confident** that we shouldn't differentiate between server and client in our RPC support, which leads to more confusion because what we're implementing is the two **together**, not either.

+    message,
+  })
+end
+
+--- Extract `content-length` from the header.
+---
+--- The structure of header fields conforms to [HTTP semantics](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#section-3.2),
+--- i.e., `header-field = field-name : OWS field-value OWS`. OWS means optional whitespace (space/horizontal tabs).
+---
+--- We ignore lines ending with `\n` that don't contain `content-length`, since some servers
+--- write log to standard output and there's no way to avoid it.
+--- See https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/35743#pullrequestreview-3379705828
+--- @param header string The header to parse
+--- @return integer
+local function get_content_length(header)
+  local state = 'name'
+  local i, len = 1, #header
+  local j, name = 1, 'content-length'
+  local buf = strbuffer.new()
+  local digit = true
+  while i <= len do
+    local c = header:byte(i)

+      if c >= 65 and c <= 90 then -- lower case

+      end
+      if (c == 32 or c == 9) and j == 1 then -- luacheck: ignore 542
+        -- skip OWS for compatibility only

+        j = j + 1

+  end
+  error('Content-Length not found in header: ' .. header)
+end
+
+local M = {}
+
+--- Mapping of error codes used by the client
+--- @enum vim.json.rpc.ClientErrors
+local client_errors = {
+  INVALID_SERVER_MESSAGE = 1,
+  INVALID_SERVER_JSON = 2,
+  NO_RESULT_CALLBACK_FOUND = 3,
+  READ_ERROR = 4,
+  NOTIFICATION_HANDLER_ERROR = 5,
+  SERVER_REQUEST_HANDLER_ERROR = 6,
+  SERVER_RESULT_CALLBACK_ERROR = 7,
+}
+
+--- @type table<string,integer> | table<integer,string>
+--- @nodoc
+M.client_errors = vim.deepcopy(client_errors)
+for k, v in pairs(client_errors) do
+  M.client_errors[v] = k
+end
+
+--- Dispatchers for incoming JSON-RPC message types.
+--- @class vim.json.rpc.Dispatchers
+--- @inlinedoc
+--- @field on_notify fun(method: string, params: table?)
+--- @field on_request fun(method: string, params: table?): any?, vim.json.rpc.Error?

comment_data.body:
~Got it. I guess that makes sense for the JSON-RPC layer.~ But, what do you think about naming this differently on the LSP layer?

> The key problem is that the rpc object we are implementing is neither a JSON-RPC client (because it can respond to requests) nor a JSON-RPC server (because it can send requests). 

I may be missing something because of my lack of familiarity with JSON-RPC, but doesn't this imply that `on_request`/`on_notify` would be an even worse name? If I'm understanding you correctly, the current `Client` implementation establishes a two-way communication were both sides act as a JSON-RPC client and a JSON-RPC server. If that's true, these methods should be called `notify` /`request`, shouldn't they?

comment_data.body: I don't plan to change the LSP layer. A simple compatibility layer for vim.lsp.rpc will be left here; changing the name will cause a breaking change, and it doesn't make sense.

comment_data.body:
You are right, I misread the LSP part of the changes. Sorry.

What do you think about my other question? Isn't using `notify`/`request` more consistent with the bidirectional communication model?

comment_data.body: The current 'Peer' object (I'm considering calling it 'Connection') already has a 'request' method, which is used to send requests, so I'm using 'on_request' here to distinguish it from 'request' and indicate that it's used to handle incoming requests.

+--- @return integer
+local function get_content_length(header)
+  local state = 'name'
+  local i, len = 1, #header
+  local j, name = 1, 'content-length'
+  local buf = strbuffer.new()
+  local digit = true
+  while i <= len do
+    local c = header:byte(i)
+    if state == 'name' then
+      if c >= 65 and c <= 90 then -- lower case
+        c = c + 32
+      end
+      if (c == 32 or c == 9) and j == 1 then -- luacheck: ignore 542
+        -- skip OWS for compatibility only
+      elseif c == name:byte(j) then
+        j = j + 1
+      elseif c == 58 and j == 15 then
+        state = 'colon'
+      else
+        state = 'invalid'
+      end
+    elseif state == 'colon' then
+      if c ~= 32 and c ~= 9 then -- skip OWS normally
+        state = 'value'
+        i = i - 1
+      end
+    elseif state == 'value' then
+      if c == 13 and header:byte(i + 1) == 10 then -- must end with \r\n
+        local value = buf:get()
+        if digit then
+          return vim._assert_integer(value)
+        end
+        error('value of Content-Length is not number: ' .. value)
+      else
+        buf:put(string.char(c))
+      end
+      if c < 48 and c ~= 32 and c ~= 9 or c > 57 then
+        digit = false
+      end
+    elseif state == 'invalid' then
+      if c == 10 then -- reset for next line
+        state, j = 'name', 1
+      end
+    end
+    i = i + 1
+  end
+  error('Content-Length not found in header: ' .. header)
+end
+
+local M = {}
+
+--- Mapping of error codes used by the client
+--- @enum vim.json.rpc.ClientErrors
+local client_errors = {
+  INVALID_SERVER_MESSAGE = 1,
+  INVALID_SERVER_JSON = 2,
+  NO_RESULT_CALLBACK_FOUND = 3,
+  READ_ERROR = 4,
+  NOTIFICATION_HANDLER_ERROR = 5,
+  SERVER_REQUEST_HANDLER_ERROR = 6,
+  SERVER_RESULT_CALLBACK_ERROR = 7,
+}
+
+--- @type table<string,integer> | table<integer,string>
+--- @nodoc
+M.client_errors = vim.deepcopy(client_errors)
+for k, v in pairs(client_errors) do
+  M.client_errors[v] = k
+end
+
+--- Dispatchers for incoming JSON-RPC message types.
+--- @class vim.json.rpc.Dispatchers
+--- @inlinedoc
+--- @field on_notify fun(method: string, params: table?)
+--- @field on_request fun(method: string, params: table?): any?, vim.json.rpc.Error?

comment_regular_data.body: Through the past days of effort, a preliminary implementation is now available in this PR, and I have developed a detailed roadmap. Since this series of refactors is more extensive than I initially envisioned, I have split them into other PRs. See the updated architecture and roadmap above.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability

title: feat(json): add generic JSON-RPC client
